mappings.rationaleAI: Theory of chemical bonding (D): ionic, covalent, metallic bonds (D.1.b), quantum-mechanical treatment (D.2), hydrogen bonding (D.3), and experimental observation (D.4).
Molecular structure theory (A), experimental procedures for structure determination (B), and classification of compounds (E).
Major groups of organic compounds including hydrocarbons, halogen compounds, organometallics (G.1).
Classification by elemental composition (E.1), bond type (E.2), and chemical reactivity (E.3).
Molecular structure theory (A.1), bonds and shapes (A.3), and relation to bulk properties (A.5).
Electronic theory of bonding (D.1.b) and molecular orbital concepts (D.2.a).
Inorganic compounds: nomenclature (F.1), structural classification (F.2), preparation (F.3), and reactions (F.4).
Experimental procedures for structure determination (B) and molecular spectra theory (C).
Early concepts of molecular structure (A.1) and quantum theory applied to atomic structure (D.1.c).
Carbon's bonding versatility underlies organic compounds (G.1.a), classified by elemental composition (E.1).
Spectrochemical methods (B.3.a) and molecular spectra (C).
DNA as a nucleic acid is covered under organic compounds (G.1.r).
RNA as a nucleic acid is covered under organic compounds (G.1.r).
Carbohydrates as organic compounds (G.1.m).
Amino acids, proteins, and peptides (G.1.n).
Natural rubber as an isoprene biopolymer connects to organic polymers (G.1.u).
Organic silicon compounds (G.1.j).
Oxygen in oxides, anhydrides, acids, and bases (F.2.b).
CO2 as a linear triatomic molecule (A.3.a) and an oxide (F.2.b).
Directly covers classification of compounds by chemical reactivity as acids, bases, and salts (E.3) and acid-base reactions of inorganic compounds (F.4), providing coverage for both outline paths.
Classification by physical state (E.4) and origin (E.5).
Water's hydrogen bonding connects to molecular bonds (A.3) and bonding effects (D.3).
Lavoisier's systematic nomenclature contributed to early molecular structure concepts (A.1).
Macromolecule classes map to organic compound categories: proteins (G.1.n) and nucleic acids (G.1.r).
Natural gas (methane and alkanes) falls under aliphatic hydrocarbons (G.1.a).
Chemical behavior determined by molecular bond nature (A.5.b).
